qa_pairs.question: Where can I find information about applying for a CCDB account?
qa_pairs.answer: If you don't already have an account, you can find information on how to apply for a CCDB account, multifactor authentication, and frequently asked questions about the CCDB.

qa_pairs.question: What should an experienced HPC user know before logging into a cluster?
qa_pairs.answer: An experienced HPC user should know what systems are available, what software is available, how environment modules work, how to submit jobs, and how filesystems are organized.

qa_pairs.question: What resources are available for new HPC users?
qa_pairs.answer: New HPC users can read about how to connect to our HPC systems with SSH, an introduction to Linux systems, and how to transfer files to and from our systems.

qa_pairs.question: Are there guides available for specific disciplines regarding software and hardware?
qa_pairs.answer: Yes, a series of discipline guides is in preparation. Currently, you can consult guides on AI and Machine Learning, Bioinformatics, Biomolecular simulation, Computational chemistry, Computational fluid dynamics (CFD), Geographic information systems (GIS), and Visualization.

qa_pairs.question: How can I transfer large amounts of data across the network?
qa_pairs.answer: If you have hundreds of gigabytes of data to move across the network, you should read about the Globus file transfer service.

qa_pairs.question: Where can Python users find information on installing modules in a virtual environment?
qa_pairs.answer: Python users can learn how to install modules in a virtual environment by consulting the relevant Python documentation.

qa_pairs.question: Where can R users find information on installing packages?
qa_pairs.answer: R users can learn how to install packages by consulting the relevant R documentation.

qa_pairs.question: What options are available for experimenting with software that does not run well on HPC clusters?
qa_pairs.answer: If you want to experiment with software that doesn’t run well on our HPC clusters, you can read about our cloud resources.

qa_pairs.question: What should I do if I have other questions not addressed in the documentation?
qa_pairs.answer: For any other questions, you might try the 'Search' box in the upper right corner of this page, visit the main page for our technical documentation, or contact us by email for technical support.

qa_pairs.question: What is my password for logging into new national systems?
qa_pairs.answer: Your password to log in to all new national systems is the same one you use to log into CCDB.

qa_pairs.question: How can I find my username for the national systems?
qa_pairs.answer: Your username will be displayed at the top of the page once you've logged into CCDB.

qa_pairs.question: Which systems are available for users to request access to?
qa_pairs.answer: You can request access to Arbutus, Fir, Narval, Nibi, Rorqual, and Trillium.

qa_pairs.question: How do I request access to the available systems?
qa_pairs.answer: You can request access to any or all of our systems by visiting the 'Access Systems' page in CCDB.

qa_pairs.question: What type of system is Arbutus?
qa_pairs.answer: Arbutus is a cloud site that allows users to launch and customize virtual machines.

qa_pairs.question: What are Fir, Narval, Nibi, and Rorqual?
qa_pairs.answer: Fir, Narval, Nibi, and Rorqual are general-purpose clusters (or supercomputers) composed of a variety of nodes, including large memory nodes and nodes with accelerators such as GPUs.

qa_pairs.question: How do users log into general-purpose clusters like Fir, Narval, Nibi, and Rorqual?
qa_pairs.answer: You can log into any of these general-purpose clusters using SSH.

qa_pairs.question: What happens when I log into a general-purpose cluster for the first time?
qa_pairs.answer: A /home directory will be automatically created for you the first time you log into a general-purpose cluster.

qa_pairs.question: What is Trillium designed for?
qa_pairs.answer: Trillium is a homogeneous cluster (or supercomputer) designed for large parallel jobs (>1000 cores).

qa_pairs.question: Why is 'cluster' often used instead of 'supercomputer' in this documentation?
qa_pairs.answer: The term 'cluster' is generally used instead of 'supercomputer' because it better reflects the architecture of our systems: a large number of individual computers, or 'nodes,' linked together as a unit.

qa_pairs.question: Who can help me choose the best system for my needs?
qa_pairs.answer: If the provided system descriptions are insufficient, you should contact our technical support team for assistance in identifying the best resource.

qa_pairs.question: What information might technical support ask for to help me select a system?
qa_pairs.answer: To identify the best resource, technical support may ask about the software you want to use (including commercial licenses, non-interactivity, and Linux compatibility), the required memory, time, computing power, accelerators, storage, network bandwidth for a typical job, and how frequently you'll run this type of job.

qa_pairs.question: What are key software considerations when choosing an HPC system?
qa_pairs.answer: Key software considerations include whether it requires a commercial license, if it can be used non-interactively (controlled from a file rather than a graphical interface), and if it can run on the Linux operating system.

qa_pairs.question: What kind of job requirement estimates are helpful for system recommendation?
qa_pairs.answer: Rough estimates for job requirements such as memory, time, computing power, accelerators, storage, and network bandwidth are helpful for system recommendation.

qa_pairs.question: What if I don't know the answers to the questions asked by technical support regarding my job requirements?
qa_pairs.answer: If you do not know the answers to these questions, our technical support team is there to help you find them, and they will then direct you to the most appropriate resources for your needs.
